Level2/3 Description: Involves operations that manipulate or combine 3D spatial objects, including union, intersection, and difference of volumetric or surface data.
Focuses on estimating values at unsampled 3D locations based on known data points, often used for creating continuous 3D surfaces or volumes.
Involves performing cell-based calculations on 3D raster datasets, supporting algebraic, logical, or conditional operations in volumetric grids.
Involves reassigning values of 3D raster datasets into new categories or ranges, often for simplification or thematic mapping.
Involves analyzing geometric and spatial properties of 3D surfaces, including slope, aspect, curvature, and other terrain-related metrics.
Involves evaluating visibility, line-of-sight, and occlusion in 3D space, supporting applications such as terrain analysis and spatial planning.
Involves handling temporary data storage to improve efficiency in spatial data processing, including creation, update, and cleanup of cache files.
Involves converting and saving spatial datasets into external formats for sharing, backup, or further use in other systems.
Involves loading and converting external datasets into the GIS environment, supporting various file formats and structures.
Involves organizing and maintaining 3D datasets, including surfaces, volumes, and point clouds, to support spatial analysis.
Involves operations for creating, maintaining, and querying spatial databases, ensuring data consistency and accessibility.
Involves organizing and handling data across multiple dimensions (e.g., time, scale, or attributes), supporting complex spatiotemporal analysis.
Involves basic tasks for handling spatial datasets, such as renaming, copying, deleting, and general housekeeping.
Involves managing fundamental GIS elements such as layers, fields, features, and attribute tables to maintain data integrity.
Involves handling cadastral and parcel-related datasets, supporting land ownership, usage, and boundary analysis.
Involves organizing and maintaining raster datasets, including storage, compression, tiling, and indexing.
Involves organizing and maintaining vector datasets, including topology consistency, attribute structure, and layer management.
Involves transforming spatial data between different file formats, ensuring compatibility across software and systems.
Involves creating or modifying spatial and attribute indexes to optimize data retrieval and query performance.
Involves creating, updating, and maintaining metadata to describe spatial datasets, supporting data discovery and quality assessment.
Involves creating and managing schematic representations of spatial data, emphasizing logical connections over precise geometry.
Involves structuring and managing GIS workspaces, including project files, data folders, and references, to ensure consistency and efficiency.
Extracting subsets of spatial datasets based on defined boundaries or regions.
Modifying raster datasets, including cell value changes, corrections, and updates.
Modifying vector datasets to maintain or correct topological relationships, such as connectivity and adjacency.
Editing the attributes of vector datasets, including adding, updating, or deleting fields.
Editing network or graph-based vector datasets, including nodes, edges, and connectivity attributes.
Extracting specific data from spatial datasets, including subsets, attributes, or features of interest.
Performing geometric operations on spatial data, including buffer, intersection, union, and simplification.
Assigning coordinate reference systems to spatial datasets without transforming coordinates.
Correcting geometric distortions or inaccuracies in spatial datasets.
Transforming spatial datasets from one coordinate reference system to another.
Converting raster datasets into vector format for further spatial analysis or modeling.
Converting vector datasets into raster format, often for overlay, modeling, or raster analysis.
Calculates the spatial concentration of features within a raster, such as point or line density.
Measures distances within raster datasets, including proximity to features or distance-based calculations.
Generates continuous raster surfaces from discrete data points using spatial interpolation methods.
Combines multiple raster layers to analyze spatial relationships and derive new surfaces.
Performs mathematical or logical operations on raster datasets to create new raster outputs.
Reassigns raster cell values based on criteria to simplify or standardize the dataset.
Calculates solar radiation values over raster surfaces for environmental modeling.
Calculates statistics for individual raster cells across multiple layers, such as sum, mean, minimum, and maximum.
Computes statistics based on a raster cell's surrounding neighborhood, such as local mean, majority, or focal sum.
Calculates statistics for raster cells within defined zones or regions, summarizing properties per zone.
Performs statistical tests on raster data to evaluate hypotheses, such as comparing means or distributions.
Analyzes relationships between raster layers, identifying correlations or spatial associations.

This category includes operations that compute statistics or indices based on a local neighborhood around each raster cell, such as local mean, standard deviation, z-score, or deviation from neighborhood values.
Analyze and process raster surfaces to derive terrain attributes (e.g., slope, aspect, hillshade, curvature) and extract or modify surface features (e.g., embankments, off-terrain objects).
Examines the shape, extent, and pattern of raster features for spatial characterization.
Organizes and manages geostatistical raster layers for analysis and modeling.
Estimates unknown values at unsampled locations using spatial interpolation methods such as kriging or IDW.
Designs optimal spatial sampling networks to support geostatistical analysis and modeling.
Performs spatial simulations to model variability and uncertainty in geostatistical data.

Identifies clustering or dispersion in spatial data, revealing hotspots, cold spots, and spatial autocorrelation. Includes methods such as nearest neighbor index, hot spot analysis, Moran’s I, General G, and incremental spatial autocorrelation.
Summarizes overall spatial distributions of features using statistical measures such as mean center, directional distribution, and standard deviation ellipse.

Builds models of spatial dependence to capture interactions and influences among features, commonly used for explaining or predicting geographic phenomena. Methods include spatial regression and geographically weighted regression (GWR).

Selects representative samples from spatial data based on geographic location or stratification rules, supporting statistical inference and spatial modeling. Commonly applied in environmental surveys and resource monitoring.
Identifies the geographic center or representative location of a set of features. Includes tools that calculate mean center, median center, or weighted centers to summarize spatial distributions.

Measures the degree to which spatial features and their attributes are correlated with each other over a given distance or spatial context. Includes tools for global and incremental spatial autocorrelation, Moran’s I, and similar analyses.
Converts addresses or location descriptions into spatial coordinates.
Constructs network datasets from vector data for analysis.
Calculates shortest or optimal paths between locations in a network.
Examines network node positions and their configuration for spatial optimization.
Combines multiple vector layers to identify spatial intersections and relationships.
Measures distances between features or identifies nearby features within a specified range.
Computes statistical summaries and distributions for vector attributes.
Analyzes movement of objects over time and space.
Examines geometric properties of vector features, such as length, area, and shape indices.
Enhances map readability and communication by annotating geographic features with text or symbols.
Addresses symbol conflicts, masking, and visual adjustments in maps to enhance overall cartographic quality.
Employs data-driven methods (e.g., map series, grids, and graticules) to automate map production and presentation.
Simplifies or abstracts geographic features at varying scales, retaining key characteristics while reducing visual clutter.
Manages the styles and representations of geographic features using symbol libraries and expression rules.
Visualizes relationships, distributions, patterns or networks using graph structures.
Corrects spatial distortions to align imagery with geographic coordinates.
Adjusts image pixel values to correct sensor or illumination differences.
Removes atmospheric effects such as haze or scattering from imagery.
Corrects terrain-induced distortions to produce planimetrically accurate imagery.
Reduces random or systematic noise in imagery to improve quality.
Applies arithmetic operations across bands, such as NDVI or band ratios.
Reduces dimensionality of imagery to highlight dominant variance components.
Combines multiple images or bands to improve spatial or spectral information.
Classifies imagery using user-defined training samples.
Automatically groups pixels into classes based on spectral similarity.
Classifies images based on groups of pixels (objects) using spectral and spatial characteristics.
Applies deep learning models to classify imagery.
Identifies areas of change between two or more images.
Extracts geographic features such as roads, buildings, or vegetation from imagery.
Analyzes spatial patterns of pixel values to characterize surface texture.
Divides imagery into homogeneous regions based on spectral or spatial criteria.
Identifies and locates specific objects within imagery.
Analyzes hyperspectral imagery to identify materials or land cover types.
Analyzes multispectral images to extract land cover and vegetation information.
Processes stereo or LiDAR imagery to derive three-dimensional surface information.

Improves brightness and contrast by redistributing pixel intensity values. Includes histogram equalization, histogram matching, contrast stretching, and gamma correction. Examples: HistogramEqualization, HistogramMatching, MinMaxContrastStretch.
Enhances visual representation of RGB, multispectral, or hyperspectral images through band combination, color balancing, PCA, or ratio transformations.
Enhances local features using neighborhood-based processing, such as adaptive histogram equalization, local contrast stretching, or neighborhood statistics.
Uses multi-scale representations (e.g., wavelet transform, pyramid decomposition) to enhance features at different spatial resolutions. Examples: wavelet enhancement, multiscale Retinex.
Enhances images using physical or sensor models, such as atmospheric correction, reflectance estimation, or sensor response adjustment. Common in remote sensing preprocessing.

Adjusts enhancement dynamically based on local features or learning models (e.g., machine learning, deep learning). Examples include deep-learning super-resolution and adaptive histogram equalization.
Filters that suppress noise and smooth raster images. Examples include mean, median, Gaussian, and Lee Sigma filters.
Filters that enhance image details and emphasize transitions, making features more distinct. Examples include high-pass, emboss, and unsharp masking filters.
Filters that highlight areas of strong intensity change, extracting object boundaries. Examples include Sobel, Prewitt, and Canny operators.
Filters that detect or enhance features in specific orientations, such as vertical, horizontal, or diagonal lines. Examples include line-detection filters.
Filters applied in the frequency domain (e.g., Fourier or wavelet transforms) to separate or enhance image components at different frequency bands.
Advanced filters that reduce noise while preserving edges or textures, often used in remote sensing for speckle noise reduction. Examples include bilateral and anisotropic diffusion filters.
